title: AI agent code has significantly higher churn than human-authored code
summary: A new empirical study constructed a dataset of approximately 110,000 open-source pull requests to investigate how autonomous coding agents contribute to real-world software projects. The researchers compared five prominent coding agents — OpenAI Codex, Claude Code, GitHub Copilot, Google Jules, and Devin — examining differences in merge frequency, edited file types, developer interaction patterns, and code longevity. The findings reveal that while agent activity in open-source projects is increasing rapidly, agent-authored code is associated with significantly more churn over time compared to human-authored code. The longitudinal survival analysis shows that agent-generated code is more likely to be rewritten, reverted, or substantially modified in subsequent commits, raising questions about the net productivity benefit when factoring in downstream maintenance costs.
whyItMatters: Higher code churn for agent-authored contributions means that raw throughput metrics (PRs merged, lines written) may overstate the actual productivity benefit — leaders need to track net retained code, not just gross output.
The comparative analysis across five major agents provides the first head-to-head empirical data on agent quality differences, which is critical for vendor selection and tool investment decisions.
The survival analysis methodology offers engineering organizations a replicable framework for measuring their own agent code longevity, enabling data-driven agent adoption policies.
recommendedActions: Track code churn rate as a key metric for agent-authored contributions alongside throughput metrics — high initial velocity that requires frequent rework may not represent a net gain.
Implement agent-attribution tagging in your version control workflow (e.g., commit trailers or PR labels) so you can run your own survival analysis on agent vs. human code longevity.
Use the study's comparative findings to inform agent tool evaluation: if you are selecting between agent platforms, request vendor-specific churn and retention data from pilot deployments.
risksAndCaveats: The study covers open-source repositories, where agent usage patterns, reviewer scrutiny, and contribution norms may differ significantly from enterprise/proprietary development contexts.
Agent detection in open-source PRs relies on bot signatures and metadata — some agent contributions may be misclassified as human-authored or vice versa.
Higher churn does not necessarily indicate lower quality in all cases — some churn may reflect intentional iteration, rapid prototyping, or evolving requirements rather than defects.
